pillar.metaDescription: Estimate how much you can borrow with our reverse mortgage guide. Compare HECM rules, costs, and payouts. Talk to a specialist free at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
pillar.h1: Reverse Mortgage Calculator: Estimate Your Home Equity Payout
pillar.quickAnswer: A reverse mortgage calculator estimates how much equity you can access based on your age, home value, and current interest rates. Most HECM borrowers age 62+ qualify for roughly 30% to 60% of home value, per HUD's principal-limit factor tables. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a free personalized estimate.
pillar.intro: A reverse mortgage lets homeowners 62 and older convert home equity into tax-free cash without monthly mortgage payments. Here is how the math works and what drives your number.
pillar.heroSubhead: Free, no-pressure estimates from licensed specialists. One call: 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: How does a reverse mortgage calculator work?
sections.body: A reverse mortgage calculator multiplies your home's appraised value by a HUD principal-limit factor (PLF) that rises with your age and falls when interest rates rise. The three inputs that move your number most are the age of the youngest borrower, the home's value (capped at the FHA lending limit of $1,209,750 for 2025), and the expected interest rate. Existing mortgage balances and closing costs are subtracted from the gross figure. Because PLFs are set by HUD and updated periodically, online estimates are directional, not final. A licensed specialist at 1-800-MEDIGAP can run your exact numbers and explain every line.

sections.h2: How much can you borrow with a reverse mortgage?
sections.body: Most borrowers can access roughly 30% to 60% of their home's value, depending on age and rates. A 62-year-old typically qualifies for a smaller percentage than an 80-year-old because the loan is expected to last longer. For example, on a $400,000 home, a younger borrower might tap around $140,000 in principal limit, while an older borrower could reach $220,000 or more. Any existing mortgage must be paid off first from the proceeds, which reduces the cash you receive. These figures are illustrative, not guarantees. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a calculation based on your home and birthday.

sections.h2: What factors change your reverse mortgage estimate?
sections.body: Five variables drive every reverse mortgage estimate: the youngest borrower's age, appraised home value, the FHA county lending limit, the expected interest rate, and the ongoing mortgage insurance premium. Higher age and lower rates both increase your available funds. The 2025 FHA limit caps the value used in the formula at $1,209,750, so homes worth more than that do not get extra credit on a standard HECM. Property type, required repairs, and any set-aside for taxes and insurance can also reduce net proceeds. A specialist can show you how each lever affects your bottom line.

sections.h2: Are reverse mortgage calculator results guaranteed?
sections.body: No. Online reverse mortgage calculators give estimates, not loan approvals or locked figures. Your final principal limit depends on a professional appraisal, the interest rate at closing, HUD's current PLF tables, and verification of your eligibility through financial assessment and HUD-approved counseling. Rates and FHA lending limits change, so a number you see today may differ at closing. Treat any calculator output as a starting point for a conversation. For an accurate, personalized estimate with no obligation, call the licensed specialists at 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free for senior financial questions.

sections.h2: Is a reverse mortgage right for you?
sections.body: A reverse mortgage can be a strong fit for homeowners who want to stay in their home, have substantial equity, and need supplemental income or a financial cushion. It may be a poor fit if you plan to move soon, want to leave the home debt-free to heirs, or can cover needs with cheaper options. You must keep paying property taxes, homeowners insurance, and maintenance, or the loan can become due. The decision is personal and depends on your full financial picture. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to talk it through with a specialist before you commit.

faqs.q: What is a reverse mortgage calculator?
faqs.a: A reverse mortgage calculator is a tool that estimates how much home equity you can access based on your age, home value, and current interest rates. It applies HUD's principal-limit factors to project your payout. Results are estimates only. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for an exact figure.

faqs.q: How accurate are online reverse mortgage calculators?
faqs.a: Online calculators give directional estimates, not approvals. Your real number depends on a professional appraisal, the closing-day interest rate, HUD's current factor tables, and your verified eligibility. Figures can shift before closing. For an accurate, personalized estimate at no cost, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: What age do you need to be for a reverse mortgage?
faqs.a: For a federally insured HECM reverse mortgage, the youngest borrower must be at least 62. A non-borrowing spouse can be younger but is not counted in the calculation. Some proprietary jumbo programs start at 55 in certain states. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm your eligibility.

faqs.q: How much equity do I need for a reverse mortgage?
faqs.a: Most lenders want you to have roughly 50% or more equity, because proceeds must first pay off any existing mortgage. The more equity and the older you are, the larger your available funds. There is no single fixed threshold. A specialist at 1-800-MEDIGAP can review your situation.

faqs.q: Do I still own my home with a reverse mortgage?
faqs.a: Yes. You keep title and ownership with a reverse mortgage. The lender places a lien, similar to any mortgage, but you remain the owner. You must keep paying property taxes, insurance, and upkeep to keep the loan in good standing. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP with any ownership questions.

faqs.q: Is reverse mortgage income taxable?
faqs.a: No. Reverse mortgage proceeds are loan advances, not income, so they are generally not taxable and typically do not affect Social Security or Medicare. They can affect need-based benefits like Medicaid or SSI if funds are held. Consult a tax advisor, and call 1-800-MEDIGAP for general guidance.

faqs.q: What is the maximum home value for a reverse mortgage?
faqs.a: For 2025, the FHA HECM lending limit is $1,209,750. Home value above that is not credited in the standard calculation. Owners of higher-value homes may consider proprietary jumbo reverse mortgages. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn which program fits your home.

faqs.q: How do I get a real reverse mortgage estimate?
faqs.a: Get a real estimate by speaking with a licensed specialist who can apply your exact age, appraised value, county lending limit, and current rates to HUD's factor tables. Online tools only approximate this. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a free, no-obligation, personalized reverse mortgage estimate today.

page.metaDescription: Estimate your reverse mortgage payout by age, home value, and rates. See how HECM math works, then get a free exact figure at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: A reverse mortgage calculator estimates accessible home equity using your age, home value, and current rates against HUD's principal-limit factors. Borrowers 62+ typically qualify for about 30% to 60% of value. Results are estimates, not approvals. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for an exact figure.
page.intro: Use a reverse mortgage calculator to ballpark your payout, then confirm the real number with a licensed specialist.

sections.h2: What inputs does a reverse mortgage calculator need?
sections.body: A reverse mortgage calculator needs three core inputs: the age of the youngest borrower, your home's estimated value, and the expected interest rate. From there it applies HUD's principal-limit factor (PLF) to project gross proceeds, then subtracts any existing mortgage balance and closing costs. Older borrowers and lower rates produce larger amounts. For 2025, home value is credited up to the FHA limit of $1,209,750. Because PLF tables and rates change, calculator output is a starting estimate, not a quote. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to run your exact numbers.

sections.h2: Why do calculator results differ from your final loan?
sections.body: Calculator results differ from final loan amounts because real approvals require a professional appraisal, the closing-day interest rate, current HUD factor tables, and a financial assessment. A home you estimate at $400,000 may appraise higher or lower, and rates can move between your search and your closing. Required set-asides for property taxes and insurance can also reduce net cash. Treat any online number as directional. For an accurate, no-obligation estimate tailored to your home and birthday, call the specialists at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: How much can I get from a reverse mortgage calculator estimate?
faqs.a: Most borrowers 62+ can access roughly 30% to 60% of home value, with older borrowers and lower rates qualifying for more. Existing mortgages are paid off first, reducing cash received. These are estimates only. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for an exact, personalized figure.

faqs.q: Is a reverse mortgage calculator free to use?
faqs.a: Yes, reverse mortgage calculators are free and require no commitment. They only produce estimates, not loan offers. For a precise figure based on a real appraisal and today's rates with no cost or obligation, call the licensed specialists at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: What information do I need before calling for an estimate?
faqs.a: Have your date of birth (and any co-borrower's), your home's approximate value, your remaining mortgage balance, and your ZIP code ready. These let a specialist apply the correct county lending limit and factor tables. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we will walk you through it.

faqs.q: Does using a calculator affect my credit?
faqs.a: No. Using a reverse mortgage calculator or getting a phone estimate does not pull your credit or affect your score. A credit and financial assessment happens only later if you formally apply. Get your free estimate first by calling 1-800-MEDIGAP.

page.metaDescription: Weigh the real pros and cons of a reverse mortgage for seniors: tax-free cash vs. fees and heirs. Get unbiased guidance free at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: Reverse mortgage pros include tax-free cash, no monthly mortgage payments, and staying in your home. Cons include upfront and ongoing fees, growing loan balances, and less inheritance for heirs. The right choice depends on your goals. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for unbiased guidance.
page.intro: A reverse mortgage offers real benefits and real trade-offs. Here is an honest look at both sides.

sections.h2: What are the main pros of a reverse mortgage?
sections.body: The main advantages of a reverse mortgage are tax-free access to home equity, no required monthly mortgage payments, and the ability to keep living in and owning your home. Proceeds are flexible: take a lump sum, a line of credit, monthly payments, or a combination. A HECM is federally insured, meaning you can never owe more than the home's value at repayment thanks to its non-recourse protection. For retirees who are house-rich but cash-tight, it can fund living expenses, healthcare, or debt payoff. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see if these benefits fit your situation.

sections.h2: What are the main cons of a reverse mortgage?
sections.body: The drawbacks of a reverse mortgage include meaningful upfront costs such as origination fees, a 2% upfront FHA mortgage insurance premium, and closing costs, plus ongoing interest and insurance that grow the balance over time. Because the balance rises rather than falls, heirs inherit less equity and must repay or sell to settle the loan. You must keep paying property taxes, insurance, and maintenance, or risk default. It can also affect need-based benefits like Medicaid. These trade-offs are manageable with planning. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to weigh them honestly.

sections.h2: Who is a reverse mortgage best and worst for?
sections.body: A reverse mortgage is best for homeowners 62+ with significant equity who intend to stay in their home long term and need supplemental cash flow. It is worst for those who plan to move within a few years, want to preserve the home for heirs, or could meet their needs with a cheaper home equity loan or downsizing. Couples should consider both spouses' ages and non-borrowing spouse protections. The decision is highly individual. A licensed specialist at 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you compare alternatives before deciding.

faqs.q: What is the biggest downside of a reverse mortgage?
faqs.a: The biggest downside is that the loan balance grows over time as interest and fees accrue, leaving less equity for you or your heirs. Upfront costs are also significant. These trade-offs can be worth it for the right person. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to weigh them honestly.

faqs.q: Can you lose your home with a reverse mortgage?
faqs.a: You can lose your home only if you fail to meet obligations: paying property taxes, homeowners insurance, and maintenance, or living in the home as your primary residence. Meeting these keeps the loan in good standing. A specialist at 1-800-MEDIGAP can explain how to stay compliant.

faqs.q: Do heirs inherit debt from a reverse mortgage?
faqs.a: No. A HECM is non-recourse, so heirs never owe more than the home's value. They can repay the balance and keep the home, or sell it to settle the loan and keep any remaining equity. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand your heirs' options.

faqs.q: Are the pros of a reverse mortgage worth the costs?
faqs.a: For homeowners who plan to stay long term and need cash flow, the benefits of tax-free funds and no monthly payments often outweigh the fees. For those moving soon or prioritizing inheritance, they may not. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for an honest, personalized assessment.

children.keyword: is a reverse mortgage a good idea

page.metaTitle: Is a Reverse Mortgage a Good Idea? | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Is a reverse mortgage a good idea for you? See when it makes sense and when it doesn't for seniors. Get straight answers free at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.h1: Is a Reverse Mortgage a Good Idea?
page.quickAnswer: A reverse mortgage can be a good idea for homeowners 62+ with strong equity who plan to stay in their home and need supplemental income. It is a poor idea if you may move soon or want to leave the home debt-free to heirs. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for honest guidance.
page.intro: Whether a reverse mortgage is a good idea depends entirely on your goals, timeline, and finances. Here is how to decide.
page.heroSubhead: Straight answers, no sales pressure. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: When is a reverse mortgage a good idea?
sections.body: A reverse mortgage is often a good idea when you are 62 or older, have substantial home equity, plan to stay in your home for many years, and need cash to cover living costs, healthcare, or to eliminate a current mortgage payment. It can also serve as a strategic financial tool, such as a standby line of credit that grows over time to cushion market downturns in retirement. If staying put and improving cash flow are priorities, it deserves a close look. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to explore whether your situation fits.

sections.h2: When is a reverse mortgage a bad idea?
sections.body: A reverse mortgage is usually a bad idea if you plan to move or sell within a few years, since high upfront costs are hard to recoup quickly. It is also questionable if leaving the home debt-free to heirs is a top priority, if you struggle to afford property taxes and insurance, or if a simpler option like downsizing or a home equity line would meet your needs more cheaply. Need-based benefits can also be affected. An honest review matters. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare alternatives before committing.

faqs.q: Is a reverse mortgage a good idea for seniors?
faqs.a: It can be, for seniors 62+ with strong equity who plan to age in place and need income. It is less suitable for those moving soon or prioritizing inheritance. The answer is personal. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for an honest assessment of your situation.

faqs.q: What is the catch with a reverse mortgage?
faqs.a: The catch is that the balance grows over time, fees are significant, and you must keep paying taxes, insurance, and upkeep or risk default. It also reduces inheritance. These are manageable with planning. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand every detail before deciding.

faqs.q: What do financial experts say about reverse mortgages?
faqs.a: Many experts now view a HECM line of credit as a legitimate retirement-planning tool when used strategically, not just a last resort. Others caution about costs and inheritance impact. Both views have merit depending on your goals. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for balanced guidance.

faqs.q: Are there better alternatives to a reverse mortgage?
faqs.a: Possibly. Alternatives include a home equity loan or HELOC, downsizing to a smaller home, or refinancing. Each has different costs and trade-offs. The best choice depends on your timeline and goals. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare your options at no cost.

children.keyword: reverse mortgage vs home equity loan

page.metaTitle: Reverse Mortgage vs Home Equity Loan | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Reverse mortgage vs home equity loan: compare payments, fees, and risk for seniors. Find the right fit. Free guidance at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.h1: Reverse Mortgage vs Home Equity Loan
page.quickAnswer: A reverse mortgage requires no monthly payments and is repaid when you leave the home, while a home equity loan has fixed monthly payments and a set term. Reverse mortgages need no income to qualify; home equity loans do. The right choice depends on cash flow and timeline. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: Both tap your home equity, but they work very differently. Here is a clear side-by-side comparison.

sections.h2: How do reverse mortgages and home equity loans differ?
sections.body: The core difference is repayment. A home equity loan gives you a lump sum that you repay in fixed monthly installments over a set term, like a second mortgage. A reverse mortgage requires no monthly payments; the balance grows and is repaid only when you sell, move out, or pass away. Home equity loans require qualifying income and good credit, while reverse mortgages have no income-based payment requirement (though a financial assessment confirms you can cover taxes and insurance). Reverse mortgages are limited to age 62+; home equity loans are not. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare for your case.

sections.h2: Which is cheaper, a reverse mortgage or home equity loan?
sections.body: Home equity loans generally have lower upfront costs and interest rates than reverse mortgages, which carry origination fees and FHA mortgage insurance premiums. However, the home equity loan's monthly payment strains a fixed retirement income, and the loan must be repaid even if your circumstances change. A reverse mortgage costs more upfront but frees monthly cash flow. The cheaper option depends on how long you stay and whether monthly payments are affordable. A specialist at 1-800-MEDIGAP can model both side by side for your numbers.

faqs.q: Is a reverse mortgage better than a home equity loan?
faqs.a: Neither is universally better. A reverse mortgage suits seniors who want no monthly payments and plan to stay in the home. A home equity loan suits those who can afford payments and want lower costs. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to find your best fit.

faqs.q: Do you make monthly payments on a reverse mortgage?
faqs.a: No. Unlike a home equity loan, a reverse mortgage requires no monthly mortgage payments. You still must pay property taxes, insurance, and maintenance. The loan balance is repaid when you sell, move out, or pass away. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for details.

faqs.q: Which is easier to qualify for?
faqs.a: Reverse mortgages are often easier for retirees because they have no payment-based income requirement, only a financial assessment. Home equity loans require qualifying income and good credit to support monthly payments. Age 62+ is required for a reverse mortgage. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check eligibility.

faqs.q: Can I switch from a home equity loan to a reverse mortgage?
faqs.a: Often yes. A reverse mortgage can pay off an existing home equity loan or HELOC, eliminating that monthly payment, as long as you have enough equity and meet HECM requirements. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see whether consolidating into a reverse mortgage makes sense.

page.metaDescription: Understand reverse mortgage costs and fees: origination, FHA insurance, closing costs, and interest. Get a clear breakdown free at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: Reverse mortgage costs include an origination fee, a 2% upfront FHA mortgage insurance premium, third-party closing costs, plus ongoing interest and 0.5% annual mortgage insurance. Most fees can be financed into the loan. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a full, itemized cost breakdown.
page.intro: Reverse mortgages carry several costs. Here is exactly what you pay, upfront and over time.
page.heroSubhead: Get an itemized cost breakdown. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What are the upfront costs of a reverse mortgage?
sections.body: Upfront reverse mortgage costs include the origination fee, capped by HUD at $6,000, calculated as 2% of the first $200,000 of home value plus 1% of the amount above that. You also pay an initial FHA mortgage insurance premium of 2% of the home's appraised value (or lending limit), plus third-party closing costs for appraisal, title, recording, and counseling. Most of these costs can be rolled into the loan rather than paid out of pocket, though that increases your balance. A specialist at 1-800-MEDIGAP can itemize every charge for your specific home.

sections.h2: What are the ongoing costs of a reverse mortgage?
sections.body: Ongoing reverse mortgage costs are interest charged on your growing balance and an annual FHA mortgage insurance premium of 0.5% of the loan balance. Interest can be fixed or adjustable; with adjustable rates, only drawn funds accrue interest. Some loans charge a modest monthly servicing fee. Because no payments are made, these costs compound and increase the balance over time. You also remain responsible for property taxes, homeowners insurance, and maintenance. Understanding the long-term compounding is key. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see projected balances over time.

faqs.q: How much does a reverse mortgage cost?
faqs.a: Total upfront costs often run several thousand to over ten thousand dollars, including origination (capped at $6,000), a 2% upfront FHA insurance premium, and closing costs. Ongoing costs are interest plus 0.5% annual insurance. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for an itemized estimate for your home.

faqs.q: Can reverse mortgage fees be financed into the loan?
faqs.a: Yes. Most reverse mortgage fees, including origination, insurance premiums, and closing costs, can be rolled into the loan so you pay little or nothing out of pocket. This increases your balance over time. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to weigh financing versus paying upfront.

faqs.q: What is the FHA mortgage insurance premium?
faqs.a: On a HECM, you pay an upfront FHA mortgage insurance premium of 2% of the appraised value or lending limit, plus 0.5% annually on the balance. This insurance funds the non-recourse protection, ensuring you never owe more than the home's value. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn more.

faqs.q: Are reverse mortgage interest rates fixed or variable?
faqs.a: Both options exist. Fixed-rate HECMs require a single lump-sum draw, while adjustable-rate HECMs allow a line of credit or monthly payments and charge interest only on funds you draw. The right choice depends on how you use the money. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for guidance.

faqs.q: Are there low-cost reverse mortgage options?
faqs.a: Some lenders offer reduced or lender-credit origination fees, and comparing offers can lower your total cost. Costs vary by lender and loan structure, so shopping matters. We never quote fixed carrier prices, but we can help you compare. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to review options.

page.metaDescription: Buy a new home with a reverse mortgage for purchase (HECM for Purchase). No monthly payments, age 62+. See how it works at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: A reverse mortgage for purchase, called HECM for Purchase (H4P), lets buyers 62+ purchase a new primary home using reverse mortgage proceeds plus a down payment, with no monthly mortgage payments. You combine a down payment (often 45% to 70%) with loan funds. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn how.
page.intro: You can use a reverse mortgage to buy your next home, not just tap equity in your current one. Here is how it works.
page.heroSubhead: Buy your next home with no monthly payment. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: How does a reverse mortgage for purchase work?
sections.body: A HECM for Purchase lets a buyer 62 or older combine a one-time down payment with reverse mortgage proceeds to buy a new primary residence in a single transaction, with no monthly mortgage payments afterward. The required down payment, typically 45% to 70% of the purchase price, depends on the youngest buyer's age and current rates; older buyers put down less. The reverse mortgage covers the rest. This lets seniors downsize, move closer to family, or buy an age-friendly home while preserving cash. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see your required down payment.

sections.h2: Why use a reverse mortgage to buy a home?
sections.body: Buyers use a HECM for Purchase to acquire a more suitable home while keeping more savings liquid and avoiding a monthly mortgage payment that would strain a fixed income. Instead of paying all cash and depleting reserves, or taking a traditional mortgage with monthly payments, you bridge the gap with reverse mortgage funds. It is popular for relocating near family, moving to a single-story or low-maintenance home, or right-sizing in retirement. You still pay taxes, insurance, and upkeep. A specialist at 1-800-MEDIGAP can model the numbers for your target home.

faqs.q: Can you buy a house with a reverse mortgage?
faqs.a: Yes. The HECM for Purchase program lets buyers 62+ purchase a new primary residence using a down payment plus reverse mortgage proceeds, with no monthly mortgage payments. It is a single closing. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn how it works for your purchase.

faqs.q: How much down payment is needed for a HECM for Purchase?
faqs.a: Required down payments typically range from about 45% to 70% of the purchase price, depending on the youngest buyer's age and current interest rates. Older buyers generally put down less. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for an estimate based on your age and target home.

faqs.q: What types of homes qualify for a reverse mortgage purchase?
faqs.a: Eligible properties include single-family homes, FHA-approved condos, manufactured homes meeting FHA standards, and two-to-four-unit homes where you occupy one unit. The home must be your primary residence. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm whether your prospective home qualifies.

faqs.q: Do I make payments on a HECM for Purchase?
faqs.a: No monthly mortgage payments are required. You must still pay property taxes, homeowners insurance, HOA dues, and maintenance to keep the loan in good standing. The balance is repaid when you sell, move, or pass away. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for full details.

page.metaDescription: A reverse mortgage line of credit grows over time and is yours to draw as needed. See how the HECM LOC works. Free guidance at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: A reverse mortgage line of credit (HECM LOC) gives borrowers 62+ a flexible credit line they draw from as needed, with no monthly payments. The unused portion grows over time at the loan's rate, increasing future borrowing power. Interest accrues only on funds drawn. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn more.
page.intro: The line-of-credit option is one of the most powerful and flexible ways to use a reverse mortgage. Here is why.
page.heroSubhead: A growing credit line for retirement. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: How does a reverse mortgage line of credit work?
sections.body: A reverse mortgage line of credit lets you borrow only what you need, when you need it, and interest accrues only on the amount drawn. Its standout feature is growth: the unused portion of the credit line increases over time at the same rate charged on the loan, so your available funds can grow substantially the longer you wait to use them. This makes it a flexible reserve for emergencies, healthcare, or income gaps. Unlike a HELOC, the lender cannot freeze or reduce it as long as you meet your obligations. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see your potential line.

sections.h2: Why is the growing credit line valuable in retirement?
sections.body: The growth feature makes a reverse mortgage line of credit a strategic retirement tool. A line opened early, even if untouched, expands year after year, giving you more borrowing power later when costs may rise. Retirees use it as a standby buffer to avoid selling investments during market downturns, to fund long-term care, or to bridge income gaps. Because it is non-recourse and cannot be canceled by the lender while you stay compliant, it offers reliable, flexible access to your equity. A specialist at 1-800-MEDIGAP can show how the line could grow for you.

faqs.q: Does a reverse mortgage line of credit really grow?
faqs.a: Yes. The unused portion of a HECM line of credit grows over time at the same rate applied to the loan balance, increasing your available funds the longer you leave it untouched. This growth is a key advantage over a traditional HELOC. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn more.

faqs.q: Can the lender freeze a reverse mortgage line of credit?
faqs.a: No. Unlike a HELOC, a reverse mortgage line of credit cannot be frozen or reduced by the lender as long as you meet your obligations and the loan remains in good standing. This reliability makes it valuable for retirement planning. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for details.

faqs.q: Do I pay interest on the full credit line?
faqs.a: No. Interest accrues only on the funds you actually draw, not on the entire available line. Money you leave untouched costs you nothing in interest and actually grows your borrowing power. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand how the line works.

faqs.q: Is a reverse mortgage line of credit better than a lump sum?
faqs.a: For many borrowers, yes. A line of credit offers flexibility, interest only on funds used, and a growing available balance, while a lump sum charges interest on the full amount immediately. The best choice depends on your needs. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare.

children.keyword: what happens to reverse mortgage when you die

page.metaTitle: What Happens to a Reverse Mortgage When You Die | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: When you die, heirs can repay or sell to settle a reverse mortgage. It is non-recourse, so they never owe more than the home's value. Learn more at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.h1: What Happens to a Reverse Mortgage When You Die
page.quickAnswer: When you die, a reverse mortgage becomes due. Heirs can repay the balance and keep the home, sell it and keep any leftover equity, or sign the deed to the lender. Because a HECM is non-recourse, heirs never owe more than the home's value. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for guidance.
page.intro: Understanding what happens to your reverse mortgage after death helps you and your heirs plan with confidence.
page.heroSubhead: Protect your heirs. Get answers at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What are your heirs' options after death?
sections.body: When the last borrower passes away, the reverse mortgage becomes due and payable, and heirs typically have several months (initially six, with possible extensions) to act. They have three choices: repay the loan balance, often by refinancing, and keep the home; sell the home, pay off the loan, and keep any remaining equity; or, if the home is worth less than the balance, sign over the deed and walk away owing nothing thanks to non-recourse protection. Heirs should contact the loan servicer promptly. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to help your family understand these options in advance.

sections.h2: What protections do non-borrowing spouses have?
sections.body: An eligible non-borrowing spouse can usually remain in the home after the borrowing spouse dies, even though the loan is in the deceased spouse's name, provided HUD's deferral conditions are met. These include being married at closing, being named in the loan documents, continuing to live in the home as a primary residence, and keeping taxes and insurance current. No new funds are available during deferral, but the spouse is not forced to move. Rules are specific, so plan ahead. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm how non-borrowing spouse protections apply to you.

faqs.q: What happens to a reverse mortgage when the borrower dies?
faqs.a: The loan becomes due. Heirs can repay it and keep the home, sell it and keep leftover equity, or surrender the deed. Because a HECM is non-recourse, heirs never owe more than the home's value. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to help your family plan ahead.

faqs.q: Do heirs have to pay off a reverse mortgage?
faqs.a: Heirs must settle the loan to keep the home, usually by repaying or refinancing the balance. If they do not want the home, they can sell it or surrender it with no personal liability beyond the home's value. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for details.

faqs.q: How long do heirs have to settle a reverse mortgage?
faqs.a: Heirs generally have six months after the borrower's death to repay or sell, with up to two 90-day extensions if they are actively working to resolve it. They should contact the servicer quickly. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand the timeline.

faqs.q: Can my spouse stay in the home if I die?
faqs.a: Often yes. An eligible non-borrowing spouse can typically remain in the home under HUD's deferral rules if they were married at closing, named in the documents, and keep the home as their residence with taxes and insurance paid. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm.

faqs.q: What if the home is worth less than the loan balance?
faqs.a: Because a HECM is non-recourse, neither you nor your heirs ever owe more than the home's value. If the balance exceeds the home's worth, FHA insurance covers the difference, and heirs can surrender the home owing nothing extra. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn more.

page.metaDescription: How to choose the best reverse mortgage lender: NRMLA membership, transparent fees, and strong service. Compare smartly. Free help at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: The best reverse mortgage lenders are NRMLA members with transparent fees, strong customer reviews, HUD/FHA approval, and clear, no-pressure counseling. Rather than chasing a single name, compare multiple offers on rate, costs, and service. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for unbiased help comparing lenders.
page.intro: Choosing the right reverse mortgage lender matters as much as the loan itself. Here is how to evaluate them.
page.heroSubhead: Compare lenders the smart way. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: How do you choose the best reverse mortgage lender?
sections.body: Choose a reverse mortgage lender by checking five things: membership in the National Reverse Mortgage Lenders Association (NRMLA) and its code of ethics, FHA/HUD approval to originate HECMs, transparent and itemized fee disclosures, strong independent customer reviews, and a no-pressure, education-first sales approach. Compare at least two or three offers, since rates, margins, and origination fees vary meaningfully between lenders and can change your payout. Avoid anyone who rushes you or bundles questionable products. We never quote fixed carrier prices, but we help you compare. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for unbiased guidance.

sections.h2: What red flags should you avoid in a lender?
sections.body: Watch for high-pressure sales tactics, lenders who push you to buy annuities or insurance with your loan proceeds, vague or missing fee breakdowns, and anyone discouraging you from independent HUD counseling. Reputable lenders welcome questions, encourage counseling, and put costs in writing. Be cautious of unsolicited offers and claims that sound too good to be true, since reverse mortgages are a target for scams against seniors. Always verify FHA approval and read reviews. For a trustworthy starting point and help vetting lenders, call 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free for senior financial questions.

faqs.q: Who are the best reverse mortgage lenders?
faqs.a: The best lenders are FHA-approved NRMLA members with transparent fees, strong reviews, and education-first service. Rather than a single name, the best lender for you is the one offering the best combination of rate, cost, and service. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for unbiased comparison help.

faqs.q: How do I compare reverse mortgage lenders?
faqs.a: Compare lenders on interest rate and margin, origination and closing fees, available payout options, customer reviews, and NRMLA membership. Get itemized quotes from at least two or three. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we will help you compare offers side by side at no cost.

faqs.q: Are reverse mortgage lenders regulated?
faqs.a: Yes. HECM lenders must be FHA-approved and follow HUD rules and federal lending laws, including required independent counseling and disclosure standards. Many also belong to NRMLA and follow its code of ethics. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm a lender's standing.

faqs.q: How do I avoid reverse mortgage scams?
faqs.a: Avoid scams by working only with FHA-approved lenders, refusing high-pressure pitches, never using loan proceeds to buy annuities or insurance you do not need, and completing independent HUD counseling. Verify credentials before signing. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for safe, trustworthy guidance.

children.keyword: how much can i get from a reverse mortgage

page.metaTitle: How Much Can I Get From a Reverse Mortgage? | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: How much can you get from a reverse mortgage? Usually 30% to 60% of home value based on age and rates. Get your exact figure free at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.h1: How Much Can I Get From a Reverse Mortgage?
page.quickAnswer: Most borrowers can get about 30% to 60% of their home's value from a reverse mortgage, based on the youngest borrower's age, current interest rates, and the FHA lending limit of $1,209,750 for 2025. Older borrowers and lower rates yield more. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for your exact figure.
page.intro: Your reverse mortgage payout depends on a few key factors. Here is what determines how much cash you can access.

sections.h2: What determines how much you can borrow?
sections.body: Three main factors set your reverse mortgage amount: the age of the youngest borrower, your home's appraised value (credited up to the 2025 FHA limit of $1,209,750), and the expected interest rate. HUD applies a principal-limit factor that rises with age and falls as rates rise, so a 75-year-old qualifies for more than a 62-year-old on the same home. Any existing mortgage must be paid off from the proceeds first, reducing the cash you net. Required set-asides for taxes and insurance can also lower your usable funds. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to calculate your personalized amount.

sections.h2: How can you maximize your reverse mortgage payout?
sections.body: You can increase your available funds by waiting until you are older, since principal-limit factors grow with age, and by applying when interest rates are lower. Paying down or eliminating an existing mortgage before applying leaves more cash available to you. Choosing the right product, such as an adjustable-rate line of credit that grows over time, can also expand your long-term access to equity. Higher home value helps, up to the FHA limit. Strategy matters here. A specialist at 1-800-MEDIGAP can show you how to structure the loan to maximize your benefit.

faqs.q: How much money can I get from a reverse mortgage?
faqs.a: Typically 30% to 60% of your home's value, depending on your age, current rates, and the FHA lending limit. Older borrowers and lower rates produce larger amounts. Existing mortgages reduce net cash. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for an exact, personalized figure.

faqs.q: Does my age affect how much I can borrow?
faqs.a: Yes. The older the youngest borrower, the larger the percentage of home value you can access, because the loan is expected to last fewer years. A 75-year-old qualifies for more than a 62-year-old on the same home. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for your estimate.

faqs.q: Can I get my reverse mortgage as a lump sum?
faqs.a: Yes. You can take proceeds as a lump sum, a line of credit, monthly payments, or a combination. A fixed-rate HECM requires a single lump sum, while adjustable-rate loans offer more flexible options. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to choose the right structure for you.

faqs.q: What reduces the amount I receive?
faqs.a: Your net proceeds are reduced by any existing mortgage balance that must be paid off, upfront fees and insurance if financed, and required set-asides for property taxes and insurance. Higher rates also lower your principal limit. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see your net figure.

page.metaDescription: HECM reverse mortgage requirements: age 62+, primary residence, sufficient equity, and HUD counseling. See if you qualify. Free check at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: HECM reverse mortgage requirements include being at least 62, owning your home as your primary residence with substantial equity, completing HUD-approved counseling, and passing a financial assessment showing you can pay taxes and insurance. The home must meet FHA standards. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check eligibility.
page.intro: The HECM is the most common reverse mortgage, federally insured by the FHA. Here are the exact requirements to qualify.
page.heroSubhead: See if you qualify free. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: Who qualifies for a HECM reverse mortgage?
sections.body: To qualify for a HECM, the youngest borrower must be at least 62, the home must be your primary residence, and you must own it outright or have significant equity. You must complete mandatory counseling with a HUD-approved agency and pass a financial assessment confirming you can keep up with property taxes, homeowners insurance, and maintenance. Eligible property types include single-family homes, FHA-approved condos, qualifying manufactured homes, and two-to-four-unit homes you partly occupy. The home must meet FHA condition standards or have repairs completed. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm whether you and your home qualify.

sections.h2: What is the HECM financial assessment?
sections.body: The financial assessment is a review lenders conduct to confirm you can sustain your homeownership obligations, even though a HECM has no monthly mortgage payment. They examine your income, assets, and credit history, including your track record paying property taxes and insurance. If the review raises concerns, the lender may require a Life Expectancy Set-Aside (LESA), where part of your proceeds is reserved to cover future taxes and insurance. This protects you from default. It is not a pass/fail credit score test like a traditional mortgage. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand how the assessment applies to you.

faqs.q: What are the requirements for a HECM reverse mortgage?
faqs.a: You must be 62 or older, occupy the home as your primary residence, hold substantial equity, complete HUD counseling, and pass a financial assessment for taxes and insurance. The home must meet FHA standards. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm your eligibility.

faqs.q: Do I need good credit for a HECM?
faqs.a: Not in the traditional sense. There is no minimum credit score, but a financial assessment reviews your history of paying property taxes and insurance. Problems may trigger a set-aside rather than denial. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn how your situation is evaluated.

faqs.q: What types of homes qualify for a HECM?
faqs.a: Single-family homes, FHA-approved condominiums, qualifying manufactured homes, and two-to-four-unit properties where you occupy one unit are eligible. The home must be your primary residence and meet FHA condition standards. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check whether your home qualifies.

faqs.q: Is counseling required for a HECM?
faqs.a: Yes. HUD requires every HECM applicant to complete counseling with an independent, HUD-approved agency before the loan can close. This ensures you understand the costs, alternatives, and obligations. It is a borrower protection. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we will explain the counseling step.

page.metaDescription: Reverse mortgage eligibility starts at age 62 for a HECM. Learn how the youngest-borrower rule and non-borrowing spouses work. Free help at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: Reverse mortgage eligibility for a federally insured HECM requires the youngest borrower to be at least 62. A non-borrowing spouse can be younger but is excluded from the loan calculation, though they gain certain protections. Some proprietary loans start at 55. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check your eligibility.
page.intro: Age 62 is the key threshold for a standard reverse mortgage. Here is exactly how the age rule works.

sections.h2: Why is 62 the minimum age for a reverse mortgage?
sections.body: Age 62 is the federal minimum for a HECM because the program is tied to retirement-age homeowners and FHA insurance rules. The age of the youngest borrower (or eligible non-borrowing spouse) drives the calculation: the younger that person, the smaller the percentage of equity you can access, since the loan is expected to last longer. So a couple where one spouse is 62 qualifies for less than a couple both in their 70s. Reaching 62 unlocks eligibility, but waiting can increase your payout. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see how your age affects your options.

sections.h2: What if one spouse is under 62?
sections.body: If one spouse is under 62, only the qualifying spouse can be a borrower, but the younger spouse may be listed as an eligible non-borrowing spouse. This provides important protection: if the borrowing spouse dies, the non-borrowing spouse can typically remain in the home under HUD's deferral rules, provided conditions are met. However, the loan calculation uses the younger non-borrowing spouse's age, which reduces the amount available. Some proprietary reverse mortgages allow borrowers as young as 55 in certain states. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to explore the best path for a mixed-age couple.

faqs.q: What is the minimum age for a reverse mortgage?
faqs.a: For a federally insured HECM, the youngest borrower must be at least 62. Some proprietary jumbo reverse mortgages allow borrowers as young as 55 in certain states. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to find out which program fits your age and home.

faqs.q: Can I get a reverse mortgage if my spouse is under 62?
faqs.a: Yes, if you are 62 or older. Your younger spouse can be an eligible non-borrowing spouse with protections to remain in the home, though their age reduces the loan amount available. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand your options as a mixed-age couple.

faqs.q: Does waiting past 62 increase my reverse mortgage amount?
faqs.a: Yes. The older the youngest borrower, the larger the percentage of home equity you can access. Applying later, when rates are favorable, can meaningfully increase your payout. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare what waiting could mean for your numbers.

faqs.q: Is there a maximum age for a reverse mortgage?
faqs.a: No. There is no upper age limit for a reverse mortgage. In fact, older borrowers qualify for larger amounts because the loan is expected to last fewer years. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see how much you may be eligible to receive.

page.metaDescription: Reverse mortgage rates in 2026 affect how much you can borrow. See how fixed vs. adjustable rates work and get current quotes free at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: Reverse mortgage rates in 2026 directly affect your payout: lower rates increase how much equity you can access, while higher rates reduce it. HECMs offer fixed rates (lump sum only) or adjustable rates (lines of credit and monthly draws). Rates vary by lender. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for current quotes.
page.intro: Interest rates are one of the biggest drivers of your reverse mortgage payout. Here is what to know about 2026 rates.
page.heroSubhead: Get current rate quotes free. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: How do rates affect your reverse mortgage in 2026?
sections.body: In 2026, your reverse mortgage rate shapes both how much you can borrow and how fast your balance grows. Because HUD's principal-limit factors fall as the expected rate rises, lower rates let you access more equity. The rate also determines the interest that accrues on your balance over time, since no payments are made. Rates change with broader market conditions and differ by lender and loan type. We never quote fixed carrier prices because they move, but a specialist can pull current numbers for you. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for today's reverse mortgage rates.

sections.h2: Should you choose a fixed or adjustable rate?
sections.body: Fixed-rate HECMs lock your rate but require taking all proceeds as a single lump sum at closing, which means interest accrues on the full amount immediately. Adjustable-rate HECMs let you choose a line of credit, monthly payments, or partial draws, so interest accrues only on funds you use, and the line of credit can grow over time. Many borrowers prefer adjustable rates for flexibility and the growing credit line, while a fixed rate suits those needing one large sum. The right choice depends on your goals. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to decide.

faqs.q: What are reverse mortgage rates in 2026?
faqs.a: Reverse mortgage rates in 2026 vary by lender, loan type, and market conditions, and they change frequently. Lower rates let you access more equity. We do not quote fixed carrier prices, but a specialist can pull current rates. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for today's quotes.

faqs.q: Do lower rates mean I can borrow more?
faqs.a: Yes. HUD's principal-limit factors rise as the expected interest rate falls, so lower rates increase the percentage of home equity you can access. Higher rates reduce it. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see how current rates affect your potential payout.

faqs.q: Are reverse mortgage rates fixed or adjustable?
faqs.a: Both. Fixed-rate HECMs require a single lump-sum draw, while adjustable-rate HECMs allow lines of credit and monthly payments with interest only on funds drawn. Each suits different needs. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to choose the right rate structure for you.

faqs.q: How can I get the best reverse mortgage rate?
faqs.a: Compare offers from multiple FHA-approved lenders, since rates and margins differ, and consider applying when market rates are lower. The lowest rate also increases your borrowing power. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we will help you compare current rates at no cost.

page.metaDescription: HUD requires reverse mortgage counseling before a HECM can close. Learn what it covers, what it costs, and how to schedule. Free help at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: Federal law requires every HECM reverse mortgage applicant to complete counseling with an independent, HUD-approved agency before closing. The session reviews costs, alternatives, and obligations to ensure you make an informed choice. It can be done by phone or in person, often for a modest fee. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn more.
page.intro: HUD-approved counseling is a mandatory, protective step before any HECM. Here is what it involves and why it matters.

sections.h2: What does reverse mortgage counseling cover?
sections.body: Reverse mortgage counseling is a required session with an independent, HUD-approved counselor who reviews how the loan works, its costs and fees, your payout options, your ongoing obligations to pay taxes and insurance, the impact on heirs, and alternatives such as downsizing or other home equity products. The counselor confirms you understand the loan and are not being pressured. Sessions can be completed by phone or in person and typically take 60 to 90 minutes. You receive a certificate required to proceed. This is a borrower safeguard, not a sales pitch. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP with questions about the process.

sections.h2: How much does counseling cost and how do you schedule it?
sections.body: HUD-approved reverse mortgage counseling often costs around $125 to $200, though fees can be waived or reduced for lower-income borrowers, and some agencies offer it free. You can schedule it directly with any HUD-approved agency; lenders must provide a list and cannot steer you to a specific counselor. The session is independent of the lender to keep it unbiased. Completing it is required before your application can move forward, so it is wise to schedule early. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we will help you understand the step and connect you to the right resources.

faqs.q: Is reverse mortgage counseling required?
faqs.a: Yes. Federal law and HUD require every HECM applicant to complete counseling with an independent, HUD-approved agency before the loan can close. It ensures you understand the costs, obligations, and alternatives. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn what the session covers.

faqs.q: How much does reverse mortgage counseling cost?
faqs.a: Counseling typically costs about $125 to $200, though fees may be reduced or waived for lower-income borrowers, and some agencies offer it free. The fee can sometimes be financed into the loan. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for guidance on the counseling process.

faqs.q: Can I do reverse mortgage counseling by phone?
faqs.a: Yes. HUD-approved counseling can be completed by phone or in person, whichever is more convenient. Sessions usually take 60 to 90 minutes, and you receive a certificate required to proceed. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we will explain how to get started.

faqs.q: Who provides reverse mortgage counseling?
faqs.a: Independent, HUD-approved housing counseling agencies provide the required session. Lenders must give you a list of approved agencies and cannot steer you to a particular one, keeping the advice unbiased. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn how to find an approved counselor.
